note_data.title: Financial statements of sole traders
keyPoints: **Income statement** shows the profit or loss made by a business over a specific period of time
**Revenue** is recorded at the top of the income statement and represents income from trading activities
**Cost of sales** includes opening inventory plus purchases minus closing inventory to calculate gross profit
**Gross profit** is calculated as revenue minus cost of sales and shows profit from trading before expenses
**Net profit** is calculated as gross profit minus expenses and represents the final profit available to the owner
**Balance sheet** shows the financial position of a business at a specific point in time
**Capital** represents the owner's investment in the business and increases with profits and additional capital introduced
**Drawings** represent amounts taken out of the business by the owner for personal use and reduce capital
**Assets** are resources owned by the business that have economic value and are classified as non-current or current
The accounting equation **Assets = Capital + Liabilities** must always balance on the balance sheet

definitions.definition: A financial statement that shows the revenue, expenses, and profit or loss of a business over a specific period of time
A financial statement that shows the assets, liabilities and capital of a business at a specific point in time
The amount invested in the business by the owner, including profits retained in the business
Money or goods taken out of the business by the owner for personal use
Current assets minus current liabilities, representing short-term liquidity

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Set out the full financial statement with correct headings, layout and calculations
Show your working clearly and state the final answer with appropriate units

modelAnswer.question: Explain two ways in which drawings affect the financial statements of a sole trader. (4 marks)
modelAnswer.answer: Drawings reduce the capital section on the balance sheet because they represent amounts taken out by the owner for personal use, which decreases the owner's equity in the business. Drawings do not appear on the income statement because they are not a business expense but a withdrawal of profit by the owner for personal consumption.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Because they are withdrawals by owner/for personal use
Mark 3: Drawings not shown on income statement
Mark 4: Because they are not business expenses/are personal withdrawals

commonMistakes: Never include drawings as an expense in the income statement — drawings are personal withdrawals, not business expenses
Students often forget to deduct drawings from capital when calculating closing capital
Never say 'profit and loss account' for A Level — always use 'income statement'
Students confuse revenue with profit — revenue is total income before deducting any costs
Never show closing inventory as an expense — it reduces cost of sales by being deducted from opening inventory plus purchases
examinerTips: Financial statements questions appear in every Paper 2 exam
Always use the correct format with proper headings and dates
Show all workings for calculations to earn method marks
Remember to distinguish between non-current and current assets/liabilities
formulas: Gross profit = Revenue - Cost of sales
Cost of sales = Opening inventory + Purchases - Closing inventory
Closing capital = Opening capital + Net profit + Capital introduced - Drawings
Working capital = Current assets - Current liabilities
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Standard format showing Income Statement (Revenue, Cost of sales, Gross profit, Expenses, Net profit) and Balance Sheet (Non-current assets, Current assets, Current liabilities, Working capital, Net assets, Capital account with opening balance, profit, drawings, closing balance)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (12 marks) — they asked students to prepare a Trading, Profit and Loss Account and Balance Sheet from a trial balance with adjustments for depreciation and bad debts
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (8 marks) — students had to calculate gross profit, net profit and working capital from given financial statement extracts
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which items appear in the current assets section of a sole trader's balance sheet
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q2 (10 marks) — candidates were required to prepare a corrected Profit and Loss Account after identifying errors in bookkeeping entries

workedExample.question: Sarah's trial balance shows: Sales $45,000, Purchases $28,000, Rent $3,600, Wages $4,500, Equipment $15,000, Debtors $2,800, Bank $1,900, Creditors $3,200, Capital $47,600. Prepare her Trading, Profit and Loss Account for the year. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Set up Trading Account with Sales $45,000 as revenue
Step 2: Calculate Cost of Goods Sold = Purchases $28,000 (no opening/closing stock given)
Step 3: Calculate Gross Profit = Sales $45,000 - Cost of Goods Sold $28,000 = $17,000
Step 4: Set up Profit and Loss Account starting with Gross Profit $17,000
Step 5: Deduct expenses: Rent $3,600 + Wages $4,500 = Total expenses $8,100
Step 6: Calculate Net Profit = Gross Profit $17,000 - Expenses $8,100 = $8,900
workedExample.answer: TRADING ACCOUNT: Sales $45,000, Less: Cost of Goods Sold $28,000, Gross Profit $17,000. PROFIT AND LOSS ACCOUNT: Gross Profit $17,000, Less: Rent $3,600, Wages $4,500, Total Expenses $8,100, Net Profit $8,900
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct Trading Account format, 2 marks for accurate gross profit calculation, 2 marks for Profit and Loss Account layout, 2 marks for correct net profit figure

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to distinguish between capital and revenue expenditure when preparing financial statements' — always check whether expenses are for the current period or relate to fixed assets

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently placed drawings in the Profit and Loss Account instead of the Balance Sheet' — remember drawings reduce capital and appear in the financed by section, not as an expense

Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often calculated working capital incorrectly by including fixed assets' — working capital is current assets minus current liabilities only, never include equipment or premises
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the interconnected structure showing: Trial Balance (top) feeding into Trading Account (Sales, COGS, Gross Profit), which flows to Profit and Loss Account (Gross Profit, Expenses, Net Profit), and Balance Sheet (Fixed Assets, Current Assets, Current Liabilities, Capital + Net Profit - Drawings). Show arrows indicating how Net Profit transfers from P&L to increase Capital in Balance Sheet, and how the accounting equation Assets = Capital + Liabilities must balance.
note_data.quickRecap: Financial statements for sole traders comprise Trading Account (calculates gross profit), Profit and Loss Account (calculates net profit), and Balance Sheet (shows financial position). Trading Account: Sales minus Cost of Goods Sold equals Gross Profit. Profit and Loss Account: Gross Profit minus expenses equals Net Profit. Balance Sheet: Assets must equal Capital plus Liabilities, with Net Profit added to Capital and Drawings subtracted.
